questions.question: Light meters are spectrally weighted to match the light-adapted human eye. Both respond most strongly near:
options: 254 nm, in the ultraviolet
555 nm, in the green
740 nm, at the red edge
1064 nm, in the infrared
questions.explanation: Photopic eye sensitivity peaks near 555 nm, and lux meters are filtered to the same response so their readings track visual effect rather than raw radiated energy.

questions.question: Compared with a standard incandescent lamp, a quartz-halogen lamp:
options: Runs its filament hotter for whiter light and longer life, thanks to the halogen cycle redepositing tungsten
Runs cool enough to use safely against solvents and coatings
Is a luminescent radiator, like an LED
Produces no infrared output
questions.explanation: The halogen fill returns evaporated tungsten to the filament, so it can run around 3000–3300 K — whiter light and longer life — but the quartz envelope gets extremely hot. It is still a thermal (incandescent) radiator.

questions.question: A two-second power blip kills the metal-halide floodlights over the yard. You should expect:
options: Full light again the instant power returns
Several minutes of cool-down, restrike, and warm-up before full output — have portable lighting ready
Permanent lamp failure in every fixture
The lamps to shift to ultraviolet output until they cool
questions.explanation: A hot HID arc tube cannot restrike until it cools, and it then needs minutes to return to full brightness — a known operational gap you plan portable lighting around.

questions.question: Which of the following is a luminescent radiator?
options: A red-hot forging glowing in a dark shop
A tungsten-filament trouble light
A white LED headlamp
A quartz-halogen work light
questions.explanation: An LED emits by electroluminescence — excited electrons at a semiconductor junction — without incandescence. The forging, the tungsten filament, and the halogen lamp all glow because they are hot.

questions.question: A procedure sets a 100 fc minimum and your meter reads in lux. You need at least about:
options: 108 lx
540 lx
questions.explanation: One footcandle is about 10.76 lux, so 100 fc × 10.76 ≈ 1076 lx — roughly 1080 lx on the meter.

questions.question: The correct way to verify lighting for a direct visual examination is to:
options: Read the lamp's lumen rating from its datasheet
Measure with a calibrated meter at the examination surface, using the working lamp at working distance, in the hardest-to-light area
Measure once at the shop entrance at the start of the week
Hold the meter at eye level facing the lamp
questions.explanation: Illuminance requirements apply at the surface to be examined; only a calibrated meter in that surface's plane, lit by the actual lamp at the actual distance and aim, proves compliance — checked where conditions are worst.

questions.question: For direct visual examination, widely used geometry limits place the eye:
options: Within 600 mm (24 in.) of the surface, at an angle not less than 30° to the surface
Within 2 m of the surface, at any viewing angle
Within 600 mm of the surface, at an angle not less than 60° to the surface
Anywhere, provided illuminance exceeds 500 lx
questions.explanation: Common direct-VT practice keeps the eye within 24 inches (600 mm) of the surface and the viewing angle no flatter than 30° to the surface, so detail and contrast stay usable.

questions.question: Aiming the same lamp so its beam strikes the surface at 60° from the normal instead of straight on:
options: Has no effect on illuminance
Doubles the illuminance
Cuts illuminance roughly in half, since cos 60° = 0.5
Cuts illuminance to one quarter
questions.explanation: The cosine law of illumination: E = (I/d²)·cos θ. At 60° from the normal the same beam smears over a longer footprint and delivers half the lux.

questions.question: The difference between illuminance and luminance is that:
options: Illuminance is light arriving at the surface (lux); luminance is light leaving the surface toward the eye — and luminance is what you actually see
Illuminance applies only to daylight and luminance only to lamps
Luminance is measured in lumens and illuminance in candela
They are two names for the same quantity
questions.explanation: Illuminance (lux or footcandles) is the light delivered to the surface; luminance (candela per square meter) is what the surface sends back toward the eye, and it is the quantity that contrast — and detection — is built from.

questions.question: A thin, even film of oil left on the examination surface mainly:
options: Improves visibility by adding gloss
Reduces luminous contrast by pulling indication and background brightness toward the same value
Matters only for penetrant testing, not visual
Raises the color rendering index of the lighting
questions.explanation: Films and contamination even out the light returned by indication and background, burying the luminance difference detection depends on — which is why pre-examination cleaning is contrast recovery.
